story:
Daisy doesn’t mean to discover Bigfoot; she’s just hunting for the good pinecones—the big, fat ones her teacher calls “excellent for crafts” and her mom calls “excellent for bugs.” She’s got a secret stash at home under her bed, beside a potato-chip-shaped rock and a wizardly stick. Then the forest detonates with a sneeze. Birds erupt. A raccoon runs by holding its ears. Daisy drops her basket and yells, “Bless you!” A nervous, rumbly voice answers, “Oh. Uh. Thanks.” Out steps a creature the size of a minivan, shaggy as twelve rugs, with feet bigger than both of Daisy’s feet put together and a nose still red from the sneeze. “Are you…Bigfoot?” Daisy asks. He winces. “I prefer Carl.”

They become instant best friends. Not that anyone believes her. Daisy tells her parents; Mom says, “That’s nice, sweetie,” and Dad chuckles about imaginary friends. Meanwhile, in the woods, Carl tries to “blend in” by crouching behind bushes while nine feet of fur sticks out. “No, you can’t,” he whispers when Daisy says she can still see him. “I am one with the shrub.”

Their first caper is the Pinecone Heist. Daisy hoards cones for crafts; Carl treats them like trophies. “Nature’s footballs,” he declares, tossing one fifty feet and catching it—until Daisy points out the ants. He apologizes to the ants, and he and Daisy build the Great Pinecone Tower of Daisyville, a fifteen-foot-tall, tipsy monument that leans like a sleepy giraffe on roller skates. It collapses across the trail; three joggers scream “BIGFOOT!” and Carl wilts. “Do I look scary?” “Yes,” Daisy says, “but in a cute way.” They rebuild deeper in the woods. The squirrels give it five stars.

On Marshmallow Friday, Carl discovers sugar. He squishes one until it oozes like toothpaste, tries to eat the second whole—bag included—until Daisy yanks the bag away, and by the fifth he’s in bliss, sticky strings of marshmallow webbed through his fur. They try roasting—Daisy’s “borrowed” lighter is a bad idea—his fur singes, he stops, drops, and rolls until it goes out, and he ends up smelling like a giant toasted s’more. The smoke attracts a park ranger, who sees Carl, fumbles his whistle, and freezes. Daisy blurts, “That’s my Uncle Larry. He…does CrossFit.” Carl flexes like a yeti popping bubble wrap. The ranger squints and says, “Tell Uncle Larry to shave once in a while,” then leaves. Lesson learned: no more fires without grown-ups and proper safety.

Still, Daisy’s parents remain unconvinced. Carl crouches outside the window to eavesdrop. “Should I wave?” he whispers. “NO,” Daisy hisses. Parents don’t always get it right away.

Everything wobbles the day Carl follows Daisy into town. He hides behind trucks, ducks into alleys, but nine feet of fluff is hard to ignore. At the ice cream stand, he whispers, “Get me twelve.” The vendor spots his shadow and shrieks, “BIGFOOT!” Chaos blooms. Moms grab kids. Teenagers snap blurry photos. Someone yells for Animal Control. A guy appears with a pool noodle like a sword. “I’m not dangerous! I just like sprinkles!” Carl pleads, snatching three abandoned cones off the counter and stuffing them in his mouth. Daisy hauls him toward the trees, but a crowd forms. Carl trips. Thinking fast, Daisy—who carries a mini craft kit everywhere—grabs a fallen “Two Scoops Tuesday” poster from the stand, scrawls “NOT BIGFOOT” with a pink glitter marker, slathers the edges with blue, scented glue from her backpack, and slaps it onto his hairy chest. The crowd reads it, squints, and decides it must’ve been some kind of promotional mascot in a shaggy costume. People don’t always look closely. Panic softens into confused grumbling, which is close enough to a crisis being averted. Mostly sticky.

Life returns to Daisy-and-Carl normal: forts from fallen branches, tag with confused deer, burping contests that rattle the porch windows. The town still jumps at shadows, her parents still praise “creativity,” and Daisy doesn’t mind. She has a best friend who’s bigger than life, hairier than a bear, and surprisingly great at hopscotch.

One golden afternoon, Carl asks, “You don’t think I’m scary?” Daisy shakes her head. “You’re just Carl.” He grins, all teeth and crumbs. “Good. Because you’re just Daisy. And you’re my favorite human.” “Even better than marshmallows?” she teases. Carl considers. “…Tie.” They laugh until the forest echoes.
